Flag H1 that appears undersized for its context — a very small H1 on a content-heavy or marketing page fails to establish page dominance
knowledge.vector_embedding_text: Typography rule: each heading level must be perceptibly larger or heavier than the level below it. This falls under the typography category, heading-scale subcategory. A well-defined heading hierarchy lets users scan page structure without reading — heading sizes are navigation anchors. Adjacent levels that are visually indistinguishable fail this purpose. Both size and weight contribute to perceived importance: H1 is typically maximum weight, with successive levels stepping down. Exact sizes depend on context — dashboards use tighter heading scales than marketing pages — so reference ranges should inform judgment, not trigger automatic flags. Optimal reference: H1 32–48px weight 700–900; H2 24–32px weight 600–800; H3 20–24px weight 600–700; H4 18–20px; H5/H6 16–18px. To detect: extract font-size and font-weight for all heading levels; flag adjacent levels within 2px of each other; check that weight decreases across levels. Full-page scope. Complements kb-vh-006 which covers the overall type scale count.

knowledge.rule: Headings must have more space above them than below. This binds each heading visually to the content it introduces, not the content that precedes it. Equal or inverted spacing makes headings float ambiguously between sections.
knowledge.detail: This is the typographic application of the Gestalt proximity principle (see kb-vh-008) applied specifically to heading-content relationships. A heading closer to the content below it than to the content above it communicates ownership — this heading belongs to this section. When the space above and below a heading is equal or when there is more space below than above, the heading appears to conclude the previous section rather than introduce the next one. Typical reference: headings need 1.5–2em above, 0.5–1em below; paragraphs need 0–1em above, 1–1.5em below.
detection_signals: Visually: check whether each heading is noticeably closer to the content below it than to the content above it — equal spacing is a concern, inverted spacing is a clear failure
Look for section headings that appear to float equidistant between two content blocks with no clear ownership of what follows
This is the same proximity principle as kb-vh-008 applied to typographic rhythm — if heading spacing matches section spacing, grouping is absent
Reference margins (orientation): headings typically need roughly 2× more space above than below to create clear ownership of the following content
knowledge.vector_embedding_text: Typography principle: headings must have more space above them than below, binding each heading visually to the content it introduces. This falls under the typography category, vertical-rhythm subcategory. This is the typographic application of the Gestalt proximity principle — elements closer together are perceived as belonging to the same group. A heading that is closer to the content below it communicates ownership: this heading introduces this section. Equal spacing above and below makes the heading appear to float ambiguously between sections. Inverted spacing — more space below than above — makes the heading appear to conclude the preceding section rather than introduce the following one. Reference: headings typically need approximately 1.5–2em above and 0.5–1em below. To detect: visually check whether each heading is noticeably closer to what follows than to what precedes it. Also see kb-vh-008 (proximity grouping) for the broader principle. Applies across both viewport and full page.

knowledge.rule: Multi-step flows, forms, and processes must show users where they are and what comes next. A user who cannot tell if an action was registered or how many steps remain loses confidence and trust in the product.
knowledge.detail: When users know the current system status, they can predict outcomes and determine next steps. Without feedback, users don't know whether their action was received, whether the system is working, or how much effort remains. This is particularly critical in checkout flows, onboarding sequences, file uploads, and form submissions — any process where the user has committed effort and needs to know their progress.
detection_signals: Check multi-step forms or checkout flows: is there a visible progress indicator — step stepper, breadcrumb trail, or 'Step 2 of 4' label?
Check interactive elements for visible state feedback — a button with no loading state, active state, or post-click confirmation gives no status signal
Flag long forms with no indication of progress or how many fields remain
Check for post-submission feedback: after a form is submitted, does the page show a clear success or failure state, or does it appear unchanged?
Look for file upload or async operations with no progress indicator — a spinner or progress bar is the minimum
knowledge.vector_embedding_text: UX usability principle: multi-step flows, forms, and processes must show users where they are and what comes next. This falls under the ux-usability category, system-status subcategory — Nielsen Heuristic 1. When users understand current system status, they can predict outcomes and determine next steps. Without feedback, users don't know if their action was received or how much effort remains. Critical in checkout flows, onboarding, file uploads, and form submissions. To detect: check for visible progress indicators in multi-step flows; check that interactive elements provide post-interaction state feedback; flag forms with no progress signal; check for post-submission success or failure states. Applies across both viewport and full page.

knowledge.rule: Labels, buttons, and icons must use vocabulary and visual conventions familiar to users, not internal or technical terminology. An interface that requires users to decode unfamiliar language or non-standard icons increases cognitive load and erodes trust.
knowledge.detail: Users arrive at an interface with vocabulary and mental models formed by their real-world experience and other products they use. When UI language reflects internal system architecture, developer terminology, or corporate jargon rather than the user's own vocabulary, comprehension requires extra effort. The same applies to icons — a widely-recognised icon (shopping cart, magnifying glass for search) maps to a user's real-world model; a novel or ambiguous icon forces guessing.
detection_signals: Check CTA and button labels for jargon or internal terminology — 'Initiate onboarding' instead of 'Get started', 'Submit query' instead of 'Search'
Check navigation labels: do they reflect user goals or system architecture? 'Module 3' vs 'Pricing', 'CMS' vs 'Manage content'
Check icon usage: non-standard or novel icons without text labels require users to guess meaning — flag unfamiliar icons used without accompanying text (see also kb-w-007)
Check error messages and system notifications for technical language — error codes, stack trace fragments, or developer-oriented messages exposed to end users
Check empty states: 'No entities found' or '404' is system language; 'No results — try a different search' is user language

Check copy for passive voice and past tense constructions — 'Message has been sent' is slower and more distant than 'Message sent'; 'Your account has been created' vs 'Account created'. Active voice and present tense signal immediacy and read faster
knowledge.vector_embedding_text: UX usability principle: labels, buttons, and icons must use vocabulary and conventions familiar to users, not internal or technical terminology. This falls under the ux-usability category, real-world-language subcategory — Nielsen Heuristic 2. Users arrive with vocabulary and mental models from real-world experience. When UI language reflects system architecture or developer jargon rather than the user's vocabulary, comprehension requires extra effort. Novel icons without labels force guessing. To detect: check CTA and nav labels for internal terminology; check icons for recognisability without labels; check error messages and empty states for developer-oriented language. Applies across both viewport and full page.

knowledge.rule: Every modal, dialog, multi-step flow, and destructive confirmation must provide a clearly visible way to cancel or exit without completing the action. A flow or overlay with no exit traps users and forces them to complete it or abandon the page.
knowledge.detail: Users frequently trigger actions by mistake — tapping the wrong button, opening the wrong modal, starting a process they didn't intend to. When an easy exit is present, users feel in control and explore with confidence. When it is absent, they feel trapped. The exit must be clearly visible and labeled, not hidden in a corner, styled as a low-contrast ghost button, or absent entirely. Cancel and primary action buttons must also be visually distinct so users can confidently identify the escape path.
detection_signals: Check modals and dialogs: is there a clearly visible close button (X) and/or a Cancel button? An overlay with only a primary action and no exit is a failure
Check multi-step flows: is there a Back button or Cancel link at each step? A wizard with only a Next button traps users mid-flow
Flag forms or checkout flows where the only visible action is 'Submit' or 'Continue' with no escape path
Check that Cancel and Confirm/Submit buttons are visually distinct — if both look identical, users cannot confidently identify the safe exit
Check if the Cancel action is styled as a destructive or danger button — the exit path should never look more alarming than the action itself
knowledge.vector_embedding_text: UX usability rule: every modal, dialog, multi-step flow, and destructive confirmation must provide a clearly visible exit. This falls under the ux-usability category, user-control subcategory — Nielsen Heuristic 3. Users frequently trigger actions by mistake. When an easy exit is present, they feel in control; when absent, they feel trapped. The exit must be clearly visible and labeled — not hidden, low-contrast, or absent. Cancel and primary action buttons must be visually distinct. To detect: check modals for close buttons and cancel options; check multi-step flows for back/cancel at each step; flag flows where the only visible action is proceed with no escape; check visual distinction between Cancel and Submit. Applies across both viewport and full page.

knowledge.rule: Standard UI elements — logo, primary navigation, search, primary CTA — must appear in positions users expect based on their experience with other products. Unconventional placement forces users to relearn the interface and increases cognitive load.
knowledge.detail: Jakob's Law states that users spend most of their time on products other than yours, so their expectations are shaped by the cumulative experience of every other interface they've used. Standard placements have become conventions precisely because they work — users can find navigation, search, and identity elements without hunting. Violating these conventions is not inherently wrong, but the cost must be justified by a clear benefit. Most AI-generated UIs violate conventions accidentally, not intentionally.
detection_signals: Check logo placement: conventionally top-left (desktop) or top-center — a logo in a non-standard position is a discovery concern
Check primary navigation: conventionally a top horizontal bar (desktop) or hamburger/bottom bar (mobile) — navigation buried mid-page or only in the footer is non-standard
Check search affordance: conventionally top-right or within the navigation bar — search placed in unexpected locations requires users to hunt
Check primary CTA placement in the hero: conventionally visible above the fold, left-aligned or centered — a primary CTA below the fold with no above-fold hook is a conversion risk
This is a principle — flag only when placement is clearly unconventional and not justified by visible design intent
knowledge.vector_embedding_text: UX usability principle: standard UI elements — logo, navigation, search, primary CTA — must appear in positions users expect based on experience with other products. This falls under the ux-usability category, platform-conventions subcategory — Nielsen Heuristic 4, Jakob's Law. Users spend most of their time on other products, so their expectations are shaped by accumulated interface experience. Violating conventions forces relearning and increases cognitive load. Standard placements: logo top-left or top-center; navigation top bar (desktop) or hamburger/bottom bar (mobile); search top-right; primary CTA above the fold. To detect: check placement of logo, navigation, search, and primary CTA against standard positions. Flag only clearly unconventional placements, not deliberate creative choices. Viewport-scoped.

knowledge.rule: Irreversible or high-consequence actions must require explicit confirmation before execution. Unavailable options should be visibly disabled — not hidden — so users understand what is possible without having to discover it through failure.
knowledge.detail: The best error message is one that never needs to appear. Good error prevention removes the possibility of the error rather than recovering from it. Two distinct approaches: eliminating error-prone conditions (greying out unavailable options, constraining date pickers to valid ranges), and confirming before irreversible actions (delete confirmation dialogs, subscription cancellation warnings). Hiding unavailable options rather than disabling them creates 'mystery missing features' — users assume the product doesn't support something it actually does.
detection_signals: Check destructive actions (Delete, Remove, Cancel subscription, Clear data): do they trigger a confirmation step before execution? Single-click permanent actions with no confirmation are failures
Check date pickers and booking UIs: unavailable dates should be visibly greyed out, not absent from the calendar
Look for form inputs that accept invalid values with no inline constraint — a phone number field that accepts letters, or a date field that accepts impossible dates, increases error likelihood
Flag forms that allow submission with no visible client-side validation — sending users to a server error state when the issue could have been caught at input
Check if unavailable features or locked tiers are hidden entirely — greyed-out with explanation is preferable to invisible
knowledge.vector_embedding_text: UX usability rule: irreversible actions require explicit confirmation; unavailable options should be visibly disabled, not hidden. This falls under the ux-usability category, error-prevention subcategory — Nielsen Heuristic 5. The best error message never needs to appear. Error prevention takes two forms: eliminating error-prone conditions (constraining inputs, greying out unavailable options) and confirming before irreversible actions (delete confirmation dialogs). Hiding unavailable options instead of disabling them creates mystery missing features. To detect: check destructive actions for confirmation steps; check pickers for visible disabled states; look for unconstrained inputs that accept invalid values; flag forms with no client-side validation. Applies across both viewport and full page.

knowledge.rule: Users must be able to identify where they are in the product at any point without having to remember how they got there. Active navigation states, breadcrumbs, page titles, and contextual help reduce the memory burden placed on users.
knowledge.detail: Human short-term memory is limited. Interfaces that require users to remember information from one screen or step to use it in another place cognitive burden on every interaction. Recognition — seeing information presented in the interface — is significantly easier than recall — retrieving it from memory. Navigation context (breadcrumbs, active states, section headers) and contextual help (inline instructions, tooltip explanations) are the primary tools for maintaining orientation without burdening memory.
detection_signals: Check multi-level navigation: does the active page have a visible selected or active state? Identical appearance for all nav items gives no location signal
Look for breadcrumbs in nested flows or multi-level content — a user three levels deep with no breadcrumb trail must recall their path to navigate back
Check if the current page or section is identified by a visible title or heading that anchors the user's location

Check complex or unfamiliar form fields and settings for contextual help — a field labeled 'API Key' or 'Webhook URL' with no helper text or tooltip forces users to leave the interface to understand what is required
In long single-page applications, check if scroll position or current section is indicated through sticky navigation, active section highlighting, or a progress indicator
knowledge.vector_embedding_text: UX usability principle: users must be able to identify where they are in the product without remembering how they got there. This falls under the ux-usability category, recognition-over-recall subcategory — Nielsen Heuristic 6. Human short-term memory is limited; recognition from visible interface elements is significantly easier than recall from memory. Navigation context — breadcrumbs, active states, section headers — and contextual help — inline instructions, tooltip explanations — are the primary tools. To detect: check active nav states; look for breadcrumbs in nested flows; verify the current page is identified by a visible title; check complex fields for contextual help text; look for section location indicators in long pages. Applies across both viewport and full page.

knowledge.rule: Every visible element on a page must serve the user's primary goal on that page. Decorative content, off-topic sections, and low-priority information that occupies prominent visual space competes with essential content and reduces its relative visibility.
knowledge.detail: This heuristic is not about visual simplicity of individual components (see kb-g-004) — it is about content selection and information architecture at the page and section level. Every additional piece of content a user encounters competes for attention with the content that actually matters. A landing page with a lengthy 'our story' section above the product value proposition, or a dashboard where decorative illustrations occupy the same visual weight as key metrics, fails this principle. The question is always: does this element help the user accomplish their goal on this page?
detection_signals: Identify the page's primary goal, then check whether any prominently placed element is unrelated to that goal
Look for decorative illustrations, background patterns, or ambient animations that are as visually prominent as functional content
Check if the page has too many sections of similar visual weight — a homepage with 8+ equal-priority sections gives users no signal about where to focus
Flag hero sections where decorative imagery occupies more visual space than the value proposition or primary CTA
Distinguish from kb-g-004 (visual complexity of individual components) — this principle operates at the content and section level, asking whether content belongs on the page at all
knowledge.vector_embedding_text: UX usability principle: every visible element must serve the user's primary goal on that page. This falls under the ux-usability category, aesthetic-minimalism subcategory — Nielsen Heuristic 8. This is not about visual simplicity of individual components (see kb-g-004) — it is about content selection and information architecture at the page level. Every additional piece of content competes for attention with what actually matters. A landing page with a lengthy company story above the value proposition, or decorative illustrations as prominent as functional content, fails this principle. To detect: identify the page's primary goal and check whether prominently placed elements serve it; look for sections of equal visual weight with no hierarchy; flag decorative content competing with functional content. Applies across both viewport and full page.

knowledge.rule: Error messages must be written in plain language, name the specific problem, and offer a constructive next step or solution. Generic, technical, or vague messages leave users stuck with no path forward.
knowledge.detail: An error message that only states that something went wrong is nearly useless — users already know something went wrong because they are reading an error message. The value of an error message is in identifying what went wrong and what to do about it. 'Something went wrong' is a failure; 'Your session expired — please sign in again' is correct. Technical error codes and developer-facing messages exposed to end users compound the problem by adding language the user cannot interpret. Empty states and zero-result states follow the same rule: 'No results' is incomplete; 'No results for X — try a different search or browse popular topics' is correct. Error messages should also acknowledge the possibility of a deeper system malfunction — not every error is caused by bad user input. A message that definitively blames the user's data when the fault may lie in the system damages trust and leaves users doubting their own correct input. Phrases like 'We couldn't process this — please try again or contact support if the issue continues' maintain user confidence while covering both causes.
detection_signals: Check visible error messages for specificity: generic messages like 'Something went wrong', 'Error', or 'Request failed' are failures
Flag error messages containing technical language, error codes, or stack-trace fragments visible to end users
Check if error messages suggest a next action or solution — 'Invalid email' alone is incomplete; 'Enter a valid email address (e.g. name@domain.com)' is correct
Check empty or zero-result states: 'No results found' with no suggestions, filters, or alternative paths is a recovery failure
Check error messages for blame-assigning language — 'You entered an invalid password' places blame on the user; 'That password doesn't match our records' is neutral. Flag the blame pattern
Check error messages for inappropriately casual or playful tone — 'Oops!', 'Whoops!', 'Uh oh!' as the primary error label is dismissive at a moment of user frustration
Complements kb-w-005 which covers whether errors are identified at all — this covers the quality, tone, and actionability of the message content
knowledge.vector_embedding_text: UX usability rule: error messages must be in plain language, name the specific problem, and offer a constructive next step. This falls under the ux-usability category, error-recovery subcategory — Nielsen Heuristic 9. A generic error message is nearly useless — users already know something went wrong. The value is in identifying what and suggesting what to do. 'Something went wrong' is a failure; 'Your session expired — sign in again' is correct. Technical error codes exposed to users compound the problem. Error messages must also be blame-neutral — 'You entered an invalid password' assigns blame; 'That password doesn't match our records' is neutral. Playful or casual error language ('Oops!', 'Whoops!') is inappropriate at moments of user frustration. Empty and zero-result states follow the same rule. Complements kb-w-005 by addressing message quality, tone, and actionability. Applies across both viewport and full page.

knowledge.rule: The outcome of clicking a button must be clear from the label alone or from the label read together with its immediate surrounding context. A single-word label is acceptable when the surrounding UI makes the destination obvious. It is a failure when the button is the only signal about what happens next.
knowledge.detail: Users decide whether to click a CTA within seconds, and generic labels create hesitation because users cannot predict the outcome. The fix is not always a longer label — a button saying 'Download' on a clearly labeled download page needs no more context. The failure is when the label is the only signal: a checkout flow where every step says 'Next' tells users nothing about what they are committing to. Specific verb + object labels ('Send invoice', 'Create account', 'Book appointment') are always unambiguous regardless of context. In a B2B tool, changing a button from 'Submit' to 'Send invoice' increased click-through by 18%.
detection_signals: Flag generic labels ('Next', 'Submit', 'Continue', 'Go', 'OK') only when the surrounding heading, form title, or section description does not clarify what the user is committing to
Check primary CTAs in hero sections: if the button says 'Get started' or 'Begin' with no surrounding copy explaining what that entails, it is underspecified
A button saying 'Download' on a dedicated download page is fine; the same label in an ambiguous modal with no context is a failure — assess label in context, not in isolation
Specific verb + object labels ('Send invoice', 'Create account', 'Book appointment') are always unambiguous — positive signal regardless of surrounding context
Secondary helper text below a CTA ('We'll email your client a copy') is a good compensating signal — it clarifies outcome without lengthening the button label itself
Aim for a maximum of 3 words per button label as a useful target — labels exceeding 5–6 words should be reviewed for compression without losing meaning
Flag CTA button labels clipped by an ellipsis — a truncated button label is always a failure regardless of surrounding context; 'Download re...' communicates nothing
knowledge.vector_embedding_text: Content copy rule: the outcome of clicking a button must be clear from the label alone or from the label read with its immediate surrounding context. This falls under the content-copy category, cta-copy subcategory. Users decide whether to click within seconds — generic labels cause hesitation because the outcome is unpredictable. A single-word label is acceptable when the surrounding UI makes the destination obvious. The failure is when the label is the only signal: a checkout flow where every step says 'Next' tells users nothing about what they are committing to. Specific verb + object labels are always unambiguous. To detect: flag generic labels only when surrounding context doesn't clarify the action; check hero CTAs for specificity; treat secondary helper text below a button as a compensating signal. Applies across both viewport and full page.

knowledge.rule: UI copy must be as short as possible without losing meaning or clarity. Remove words until the meaning changes. Prefer verb phrases over noun constructions. Verbose labels and instructions add cognitive load and slow task completion.
knowledge.detail: Users scan interfaces rather than read them. Every unnecessary word in a label, heading, or instruction is friction — it slows parsing and buries the signal. Nielsen Norman Group research showed that concise copy improved task success rates, and a verbose sign-up heading reduced conversions by 12% compared to a shorter equivalent. Brevity and specificity (kb-cc-001) are complementary: be as short as possible, but ensure the outcome remains clear — either through the label or from surrounding context.
detection_signals: Look for multi-clause button labels or instructions inside interactive elements — 'Click here to proceed to the next step' should be 'Continue'
Check heading and label text for redundant qualifiers: 'Please enter your email address below' vs 'Email'
Flag instructional text that restates what the UI element already communicates visually — a text input next to a 'Search' button labeled 'Enter your search term here' is redundant
Check for noun phrase constructions that compress to verb phrases: 'Completion of registration' → 'Complete registration'; 'Submission of your form' → 'Submit'
Check for filler openers in helper text and instructions: 'In order to', 'Please note that', 'You should be aware that' — these delay the actual information
Note: brevity must not override clarity — a shorter label that creates ambiguity is worse than a longer one that is specific (see kb-cc-001)

Distinguish brevity from truncation — a label that is short because it was written concisely is correct; a label that appears short because its container clips it with an ellipsis is a content failure, not a brevity win. 'Short but clipped' is worse than 'slightly longer but fully visible'
knowledge.vector_embedding_text: Content copy principle: UI copy must be as short as possible without losing meaning. This falls under the content-copy category, copy-brevity subcategory. Users scan interfaces rather than read them — every unnecessary word is friction. Concise copy improved task success in Nielsen Norman Group research; a verbose sign-up heading reduced conversions by 12% vs a shorter equivalent. Remove words until the meaning changes. Prefer verb phrases over noun constructions. Brevity and outcome clarity are complementary — be as short as possible while ensuring the outcome remains clear from label or context. To detect: look for multi-clause labels, redundant qualifiers, noun phrases that compress to verb phrases, and filler openers in instructions. Brevity must not override clarity. Applies across both viewport and full page.

knowledge.rule: The same action or concept must use the same word everywhere in the interface. Mixing synonyms — 'Log in' on one screen and 'Sign in' on another — signals inconsistency and makes users wonder if the two actions are different.
knowledge.detail: Terminology inconsistency is a copy-level parallel to visual inconsistency (kb-g-001). When users encounter two different words for the same action, they apply the assumption that different words mean different things — a cognitive overhead that should never be required. This applies to action verbs (Log in vs Sign in, Delete vs Remove), noun references (Account vs Profile, Payment vs Billing), and capitalization patterns (Sign Up vs sign up). A consistent vocabulary list is the solution at the design system level; at the audit level, spotting inconsistency is straightforward.
detection_signals: Check authentication labels across the visible page: is it 'Log in', 'Sign in', or 'Login'? Flag any mix of variants
Check if the same destination is labeled differently in navigation vs in-page references — 'Settings' in the nav but 'Account preferences' in a CTA
Look for mixed capitalization of the same term: 'Sign Up' in a button and 'sign up' in adjacent body copy
Check action verbs on similar-function components: 'Delete' in one place and 'Remove' in another for what appears to be the same operation
Check noun references for the same concept: 'Payment', 'Billing', and 'Invoice' used interchangeably for the same section without clear distinction
knowledge.vector_embedding_text: Content copy rule: the same action or concept must use the same word everywhere in the interface. This falls under the content-copy category, terminology-consistency subcategory. Terminology inconsistency is the copy-level parallel to visual inconsistency. When users encounter two different words for the same action, they assume the difference is meaningful — cognitive overhead that should never be required. This applies to action verbs (Log in vs Sign in), noun references (Account vs Profile), and capitalization patterns. To detect: check authentication labels for mixed variants; check if the same destination has different labels in navigation vs in-page references; look for mixed capitalization of the same term; check similar-function components for different action verbs. Full-page scope. Extends kb-g-001 (visual consistency) to the copy dimension.

knowledge.rule: Copy tone must match the emotional context of the moment. Error and failure states require empathy and neutrality. Success states can be celebratory. Complex or high-stakes flows require calm, direct language. Playful or casual tone in failure states increases frustration.
knowledge.detail: Tone communicates the product's attitude toward the user at a specific moment. When a user encounters an error, they are already frustrated — a casual or humorous error message ('Oopsie! Something broke') compounds that frustration and signals that the product is not taking the problem seriously. In one documented case, a quirky error message doubled support requests. Conversely, a success state that is overly terse ('Done') misses an opportunity to reinforce the user's achievement and build confidence. The baseline voice can be warm and approachable, but it must adapt: neutral and empathetic in errors, clear and direct in high-stakes flows, appropriately celebratory in successes.
detection_signals: Check error and failure messages for casual or humorous primary labels — 'Oops!', 'Whoops!', 'Uh oh!', 'Yikes!' as the main error heading is inappropriate at a moment of user frustration
Check error messages for blame-assigning language — 'You entered an invalid email' places fault on the user; 'That email isn't in our system' is neutral (see also kb-ux-008)
Check success and confirmation states: an overly terse response ('Done', 'OK') after a significant action like payment or account creation misses appropriate reassurance
Look for onboarding or marketing copy tone bleeding into transactional flows — enthusiastic promotional language in a checkout or settings form is tonally mismatched
Check empty states for dismissive tone: 'Nothing here!' is casual; 'No items yet — add your first one to get started' is empathetic and actionable
knowledge.vector_embedding_text: Content copy principle: copy tone must match the emotional context of the moment. This falls under the content-copy category, tone-context subcategory. Tone communicates the product's attitude toward the user. In error states, users are already frustrated — casual or humorous language compounds that frustration. A quirky error message doubled support requests in a documented case. In success states, terse responses miss the opportunity to reinforce achievement. The baseline voice can be warm, but it must adapt: neutral and empathetic in errors, direct in high-stakes flows, celebratory in successes. To detect: check error headings for casual primary labels; check error messages for blame-assigning language; check success states for appropriate reassurance; look for promotional tone in transactional flows; check empty states for dismissive copy. Applies across both viewport and full page.

knowledge.rule: A horizontal navigation bar that remains expanded on mobile viewports is a failure. At mobile widths, navigation must transform into a collapsed pattern — hamburger menu, bottom bar, or drawer — to preserve screen space for content.
knowledge.detail: Navigation is the primary wayfinding tool in any interface. A full horizontal nav bar with multiple items has no room to render on a 375px viewport — items overflow, wrap, or get cut off. The correct pattern is a collapsed navigation that reveals items on interaction. When a mobile screenshot is available, this is directly visible; otherwise it must be inferred from DOM structure.
detection_signals: On the mobile screenshot: check if a full horizontal nav with multiple visible items is still present — this is a clear failure
Check if the hamburger or equivalent toggle is clearly visible and large enough to tap (minimum 44×44px)
Flag navigation that is partially visible at mobile width — items cut off by the viewport edge with no collapse mechanism
DOM: check if a hamburger toggle or mobile nav component exists in the HTML — if the desktop nav has no mobile counterpart, there is no transformation mechanism
knowledge.vector_embedding_text: Responsive mobile rule: a horizontal navigation bar must transform into a collapsed pattern at mobile widths. This falls under the responsive-mobile category, nav-mobile subcategory. A full horizontal nav cannot render on a 375px viewport — items overflow or get cut off. The correct pattern is a hamburger menu, bottom bar, or drawer. To detect: on mobile screenshot, check if the horizontal nav remains expanded; check if a toggle is visible and large enough to tap; DOM: check if a mobile nav component exists alongside the desktop nav. Full-page scope.

knowledge.rule: Multi-column grid layouts — feature grids, card rows, side-by-side form fields, content columns — must reflow to single-column or simplified layouts at mobile widths. Fixed column counts with no responsive override render cramped or overflow on small screens.
knowledge.detail: Multi-column layouts rely on horizontal space that does not exist on mobile. A 3-column feature grid at 375px either renders columns too narrow to be readable or forces horizontal scrolling. Forms are a particularly common failure: side-by-side input fields at mobile width are cramped and hard to interact with. DOM analysis can identify fixed column structures; a mobile screenshot confirms the result directly.
detection_signals: On the mobile screenshot: check if 2- or 3-column content sections have stacked to a single column
Flag form layouts with multiple input fields side by side at mobile width — side-by-side inputs at 375px are consistently too cramped
Check card grids: if cards are still 3-across at mobile width they are either very small or causing horizontal scroll
DOM: check grid containers for fixed column counts (`grid-template-columns: repeat(3, 1fr)`) with no responsive media query override
Check pricing tables, comparison sections, and feature grids — these are the most common multi-column patterns that fail to reflow
knowledge.vector_embedding_text: Responsive mobile rule: multi-column layouts must reflow to single-column or simplified layouts at mobile widths. This falls under the responsive-mobile category, layout-reflow subcategory. A 3-column grid at 375px renders columns too narrow or forces horizontal scrolling. Forms with side-by-side inputs are cramped and hard to interact with. To detect: on mobile screenshot check that multi-column sections have stacked; flag side-by-side form inputs; DOM: check grid containers for fixed column counts without responsive overrides. Full-page scope.

knowledge.rule: No element should extend beyond the viewport width at mobile, forcing horizontal scrolling. Horizontal overflow is a fundamental mobile failure — it signals the layout was never tested at mobile width.
knowledge.detail: Horizontal scrolling on mobile is almost never intentional. The most common causes are elements with fixed pixel widths exceeding the viewport, wide tables without overflow handling, navigation bars that don't collapse, and images without `max-width: 100%`. Fixed-width elements are detectable from DOM computed styles without a mobile screenshot.
detection_signals: On the mobile screenshot: check for any content visibly cut off at the right edge of the viewport
Look for wide tables, image grids, or navigation bars that extend beyond the visible screen width
Check hero sections with large horizontally-arranged elements — oversized illustrations or side-by-side text+image layouts that don't reflow
DOM: flag elements with fixed pixel widths exceeding 480px that lack `max-width: 100%` — these will cause overflow at mobile widths regardless of breakpoints
knowledge.vector_embedding_text: Responsive mobile rule: no element should extend beyond the viewport width at mobile, forcing horizontal scrolling. This falls under the responsive-mobile category, horizontal-overflow subcategory. Horizontal scrolling is almost never intentional and signals a layout never adapted for small screens. Common causes: fixed pixel widths exceeding the viewport, tables without overflow handling, images without max-width: 100%. To detect: on mobile screenshot check for content cut off at the right edge; DOM: flag elements with fixed widths above 480px without max-width constraints. Critical severity. Full-page scope.

knowledge.rule: Heading and display text sizes must scale down at mobile widths. A hero heading sized for 1440px desktop becomes disproportionate at 375px if not reduced — it dominates the viewport and pushes supporting content and the CTA below the fold.
knowledge.detail: Large display headings are designed for the horizontal space of desktop screens. At 375px, a 56px hero heading may require 4–5 lines and fill the entire above-fold area. The solution is responsive font sizing using `clamp()`, viewport units, or media query overrides. Fixed `px` heading sizes with no responsive scaling are detectable from DOM computed styles. Reference ranges for mobile: hero/H1 typically 28–36px; these are orientation values, not hard thresholds.
detection_signals: On the mobile screenshot: check if the H1 or hero heading dominates the full viewport — if the headline alone fills the above-fold area with little room for subheadline or CTA, it is too large
A hero heading at mobile that requires more than 3 lines is a strong signal the font size was not reduced from desktop
DOM: check heading font-size values — fixed `px` with no `clamp()`, `vw`, or media query override means the size will not scale at mobile widths
DOM: `font-size: clamp(...)` or `vw` units on headings are a positive signal — they scale automatically
knowledge.vector_embedding_text: Responsive mobile principle: heading and display text sizes must scale down at mobile widths. This falls under the responsive-mobile category, responsive-typography subcategory. A 56px hero heading at 375px may fill the entire above-fold area, pushing supporting content below the fold. DOM: check heading font-size values for fixed px with no clamp(), vw, or media query override. Reference ranges for mobile: hero/H1 typically 28–36px — orientation values, not hard thresholds. On mobile screenshot: check if the headline dominates the viewport leaving no room for subheadline or CTA. Full-page scope.

knowledge.rule: Primary content and the main CTA must remain near the top of the viewport when a desktop layout reflows to mobile. Secondary content and decorative elements that appear before the primary content in DOM source order will stack above the core message on mobile.
knowledge.detail: When a multi-column desktop layout stacks to single column on mobile, DOM source order determines what appears first. If a sidebar, secondary nav, or large decorative image precedes the main content in the HTML, it renders above the primary message on mobile — pushing the value proposition and CTA below the fold. DOM source order analysis can identify this risk without a mobile screenshot.
detection_signals: On the mobile screenshot: check if the primary CTA is visible without scrolling, or has been pushed below the fold by stacked secondary content
Check if a large decorative element (illustration, image, animation) stacks above the value proposition on mobile — reversed visual priority
DOM: check source order — if a sidebar or supporting section appears before the main content block in the HTML, it will render above it when stacked
DOM: check if `order` or `flex-direction: column-reverse` is used to compensate for source-order stacking — a positive signal that mobile priority was considered
knowledge.vector_embedding_text: Responsive mobile principle: primary content and the main CTA must remain near the top of the viewport when a layout reflows to mobile. This falls under the responsive-mobile category, content-priority subcategory. DOM source order determines what stacks first on mobile. Sidebars or decorative images preceding the main content in HTML will render above the primary message. To detect: on mobile screenshot check if the CTA is above the fold; DOM: check source order for secondary content appearing before the main block; check for CSS order properties compensating for source order. Viewport-scoped.

knowledge.rule: When a page provides meaningful subheadings, users adopt the layer-cake scanning pattern — fixating on headings in horizontal sweeps and reading body text only below the heading that matches their goal. Pages without subheadings force the far less effective F-pattern, where most content below the first paragraph goes unseen.
knowledge.detail: Eyetracking research identifies the layer-cake pattern as the second most effective scanning mode, just below full commitment reading. Users treat subheadings as a navigational index — they skip body text until a heading matches their need, then commit to reading that section. A page that omits subheadings or uses generic ones ('Overview', 'Details') removes this navigation mechanism entirely and forces users back to the F-pattern, where most content is ignored.
detection_signals: Check whether long text sections (3+ paragraphs) have meaningful subheadings that describe the content — not format labels like 'Introduction' or 'Details'
Flag pages where the only visual breaks in text content are blank lines between paragraphs with no heading hierarchy below H1/H2
Check if subheadings are visually distinct from body text (size, weight, or both) — subheadings styled identically to body text lose their scanning anchor function
Look for long pricing, feature, or comparison sections with no subheadings — these rely entirely on the user reading linearly
knowledge.vector_embedding_text: UX usability principle: meaningful subheadings enable the layer-cake scanning pattern, where users sweep headings like horizontal stripes and dive into body text only below the heading that matches their goal. This falls under the ux-usability category, layer-cake-scanning subcategory. Without subheadings, users fall back to the F-pattern, where most content below the first paragraph goes unseen. Pages with generic subheadings ('Overview', 'Details') or subheadings styled identically to body text lose this navigation mechanism. To detect: check whether long text sections have meaningful, visually distinct subheadings; flag pages where text breaks are only blank lines with no heading hierarchy; flag long feature, pricing, or comparison sections with no subheadings. Full-page scope. High severity.

knowledge.rule: Critical keywords within body copy must be visually differentiated from surrounding text using bold, color, or link styling. Keywords that are identical in style to surrounding text are skipped during scanning — users fixate on visually distinct words when in spotted-pattern mode.
knowledge.detail: The spotted scanning pattern shows users fixating on words that visually stand out (bold, colored, linked) or resemble what they are searching for (digits, proper nouns, capitalized terms). Products that need users to notice specific terms — a price, a feature name, a key caveat, a deadline — must signal those words visually. A paragraph of uniform-weight body text forces linear reading to locate key points. Overuse of bold dilutes the effect — only genuinely critical terms should be distinguished, 2–5 words at most per emphasis.
detection_signals: Check body copy sections for any visual differentiation of key terms (bold, color, links) — walls of uniform-weight text offer no spotted-pattern anchors
Check if numeric or pricing information is visually emphasized (bold or larger) — numbers are natural fixation targets and should be reinforced
Flag pages where bold is applied to full sentences or multiple consecutive lines — this dilutes the scanning anchor effect
Check important caveats, terms, or conditions — if these appear in uniform body text with no visual distinction, scanning users will miss them
knowledge.vector_embedding_text: UX usability rule: critical keywords within body copy must be visually differentiated using bold, color, or link styling. This falls under the ux-usability category, keyword-visual-differentiation subcategory. The spotted scanning pattern shows users fixating on words that visually stand out — bold, colored, linked — or that resemble what they are looking for. Uniform-weight body text forces linear reading; styled keywords allow targeted fixation. Overuse of bold dilutes the effect — only 2–5 critical words should be emphasized per emphasis instance. To detect: check body copy for any visual differentiation of key terms; check numeric and pricing content for visual emphasis; flag full-sentence bolding; check that caveats or conditions have visual distinction. Full-page scope. Medium severity.

knowledge.rule: Body content must be broken into short paragraphs, bullet lists, and labeled sections. Blocks of more than 4–5 continuous sentences with no visual break force linear reading and dramatically reduce comprehension — even for users who are motivated to read.
knowledge.detail: Eyetracking research confirms that even committed users — those motivated to read every word — benefit from chunked content with subheadings and bullets. A wall of text penalizes all scanning modes: F-pattern users ignore it after the first line, layer-cake scanners cannot navigate it, and spotted-pattern users find no anchors. The commitment pattern is the only mode where dense text is survivable, and it applies to only a small fraction of users in a high-motivation context. AI-generated UIs frequently produce dense feature descriptions and onboarding copy in uniform body text with no visual hierarchy.
detection_signals: Flag any continuous text block of 5+ sentences with no subheading, no bullet, and no bold keyword — this is a wall of text
Count lines in a single visual text block: more than ~6–8 lines of solid body text with no visual break is a strong signal
Check feature and benefit sections: AI-generated UIs frequently produce 3–4 sentence feature descriptions in uniform body text with no internal hierarchy
Check FAQ sections for a common anti-pattern: questions with no visual distinction from answers make the section unscannable
knowledge.vector_embedding_text: Content copy rule: body content must be broken into short paragraphs, bullet lists, and labeled sections. This falls under the content-copy category, content-chunking subcategory. Walls of unbroken text penalize all scanning modes — F-pattern users ignore content after the first line, layer-cake scanners cannot navigate, and spotted-pattern scanners find no anchors. Even committed readers benefit from structure. AI-generated UIs commonly produce dense feature descriptions and instructional text with no visual hierarchy. To detect: flag continuous text blocks of 5+ sentences with no subheading, bullet, or keyword emphasis; count lines in a single text block — more than 6–8 lines solid is a strong signal; check feature sections and FAQs for uniform body text with no structure. Full-page scope. High severity.

knowledge.rule: Proactive reassurance copy placed near forms, CTAs, and data-collection fields reduces hesitation and increases completion. Address concerns users haven't consciously voiced yet — about commitment, privacy, reversibility, and cost.
knowledge.detail: Users pause at friction points — form fields, sign-up buttons, payment flows — because of unanswered mental objections. Small reassurance lines placed in context ('You can review your order before paying', 'No credit card required', 'We never share your data', 'You can change this later') resolve those objections at the exact moment they arise. The most effective reassurance anticipates a concern the user didn't know they had. The statements must be true — false reassurance accelerates distrust when the experience doesn't match.
detection_signals: Check sign-up forms and primary CTAs: is there any reassurance copy addressing commitment level, reversibility, or data privacy near the action?
Flag checkout flows with no 'review before payment' signal or no cost/commitment transparency near the primary CTA
Check email subscription fields for spam or frequency reassurance ('No spam — unsubscribe anytime' or similar)
Look for multi-step flows with no indication that progress can be saved or that the user can go back
Check data-collection fields (phone number, address, date of birth) for a brief explanation of why the data is needed — absent context increases form abandonment
knowledge.vector_embedding_text: Content copy principle: proactive reassurance microcopy near forms, CTAs, and data-collection fields reduces hesitation and increases completion. This falls under the content-copy category, reassurance-microcopy subcategory. Users pause at friction points because of unanswered mental objections about commitment, privacy, reversibility, and cost. Reassurance lines like 'No credit card required', 'You can change this later', 'We never share your data' resolve objections at the exact moment they arise. The most effective reassurance anticipates a concern the user didn't know they had. Statements must be true. To detect: check CTAs and sign-up forms for nearby reassurance copy; flag checkout flows with no commitment transparency; check email fields for frequency reassurance; check data-collection fields for context about why data is needed. Applies across both viewport and full-page.

knowledge.rule: Opt-out and decline labels must be neutral and honest. Labels that guilt, shame, or mock users for declining ('No thanks, I don't want to improve') are a dark pattern — they manipulate rather than inform and damage brand trust.
knowledge.detail: Confirmshaming uses self-deprecating opt-out labels to pressure users by making rejection feel personally costly. It is manipulative, widely recognized as a dark pattern, and likely to damage brand perception. A related failure is deceptive opt-out labeling — a button labeled 'Skip trial' when no trial was mentioned leaves users unable to make an informed choice. Neutral alternatives ('Maybe later', 'No thanks', 'Not now') are always correct. The test: can a user click the opt-out label and accurately predict what will happen next?
detection_signals: Check dismiss and decline buttons in popups, modals, banners, and opt-in prompts for self-deprecating phrasing: 'No thanks, I don't want to [positive outcome]'
Flag any opt-out label where saying 'no' requires the user to agree to a negative self-description
Check opt-out labels for honesty: does the label accurately describe what happens when the user clicks it? Labels that reference things not established in the surrounding context are deceptive
Check cookie consent dialogs and newsletter pop-ups — the most common location for confirmshaming in AI-generated UIs
Flag two-option dialogs where the 'no' option is significantly smaller, lower-contrast, or harder to find than the 'yes' option — visual suppression of the opt-out is a related dark pattern
knowledge.vector_embedding_text: Content copy rule: opt-out and decline labels must be neutral and honest. This falls under the content-copy category, confirmshaming subcategory. Confirmshaming uses self-deprecating labels to pressure users by making rejection feel personally costly — 'No thanks, I don't want to save money.' It is a recognized dark pattern that damages brand trust. A related failure is deceptive opt-out labeling where the label references something not established in the surrounding context. Neutral alternatives like 'Maybe later' or 'No thanks' are always correct. To detect: check dismiss buttons in popups and modals for self-deprecating phrasing; check opt-out labels for honesty and predictability; check cookie and newsletter prompts; flag visual suppression of the opt-out option. High severity. Applies across both viewport and full-page.

knowledge.rule: Multi-step flows must show users how many steps remain and how long the process takes. Concealing commitment length is a friction source — users who cannot see the end of a flow are more likely to abandon it mid-way.
knowledge.detail: Unknown commitment length triggers hesitation at the start and abandonment mid-flow. A progress indicator, step count ('Step 2 of 4'), or upfront time estimate ('Takes 2 minutes') gives users enough information to commit. The most effective disclosures combine multiple reassurances: 'Free — takes 2 minutes — no credit card required.' Time estimates must be accurate; an estimate that proves wrong damages trust more than no estimate at all. Applies to onboarding, checkout, registration, surveys, and any multi-step sequence.
detection_signals: Check multi-step flows (onboarding, checkout, registration) for a progress indicator or step counter — flag when none is present
Check the entry point of a multi-step flow (first screen or CTA) for time or commitment disclosure — if absent, users have no basis for deciding whether to start
Flag survey or quiz flows with no step count or progress bar
Check that time estimates visible in the UI are plausible — '2 minutes' for a 10-field form is inaccurate and erodes trust
Look for loading or processing states with no estimated wait time — 'Processing...' with no indication of duration causes premature abandonment
knowledge.vector_embedding_text: UX usability principle: multi-step flows must show users how many steps remain and how long the process takes. This falls under the ux-usability category, progress-time-disclosure subcategory. Concealing commitment length triggers hesitation at entry and abandonment mid-flow. A step counter, progress bar, or upfront time estimate gives users enough information to commit. The most effective disclosures combine multiple reassurances: 'Free — takes 2 minutes — no credit card required.' Time estimates must be accurate — inaccuracy damages trust more than no estimate. To detect: check multi-step flows for a progress indicator; check flow entry points for time or commitment disclosure; flag surveys and quizzes with no progress signal; check processing states for estimated wait time. Applies across viewport and full-page. Medium severity.

knowledge.rule: Navigation must make the user's current location visible at all times. Users who cannot see where they are in a site or application lose spatial sense, become disoriented, and abandon tasks. Active states, breadcrumbs, and consistent navigation landmarks are not optional.
knowledge.detail: Most users cannot build or maintain elaborate mental maps of a navigation space. The web is fundamentally invisible — users can see the current page but not the structure around it. Effective navigation creates the illusion that users are always in the same place, with content changing around them. Three tools provide spatial grounding: (1) active state highlighting on the current nav item, (2) breadcrumbs or location labels for pages more than 2 levels deep, (3) consistent navigation elements on every page type — including checkout, error pages, and onboarding flows. When navigation disappears or changes between page types, users lose their sense of position entirely. For AT users, landmark labels and heading hierarchy serve the same spatial grounding function: a page with multiple unlabeled navigation regions is as disorienting for a screen reader user as a page with no active state for a sighted user.
detection_signals: Check if the current page or section is visually indicated in the navigation — active state, highlighted item, different color or weight from inactive items
Flag navigation bars where no item appears selected or active — the user has no spatial reference for their current location
Check for breadcrumbs or location labels on pages more than 2 levels deep in the hierarchy
Check whether navigation is present and consistent across all page types — checkout flows, error pages, and onboarding flows commonly strip navigation, leaving users disoriented
Look for nav items with no visual distinction between active and inactive states — identical styling regardless of current location is a spatial grounding failure

DOM: if multiple navigation regions are present (main nav + pagination, main nav + sidebar nav), check whether each has a unique aria-label — unlabeled duplicate nav landmarks are indistinguishable for AT users
DOM: check whether the main content area is identifiable via a main landmark or role='main' — AT users rely on this to skip repeated navigation
Check whether the visible page heading (h1) reflects the current page title — a mismatch between visual heading and structural identity is a spatial grounding failure for all users
knowledge.vector_embedding_text: Navigation IA principle: navigation must make the user's current location visible at all times. This falls under the navigation-ia category, visible-navigation subcategory. Most users cannot build elaborate mental maps — effective navigation creates the illusion of a stable home with content changing around them. Three tools provide spatial grounding: active state on current nav item, breadcrumbs for deep pages, and consistent navigation across all page types including checkout and error pages. To detect: check if current page is visually indicated in nav (active state, color, weight); flag nav bars with no active state indication; check for breadcrumbs on deep pages; verify navigation exists on checkout, error, and onboarding pages; check for visual distinction between active and inactive nav items. High severity. Applies across both viewport and full-page.

knowledge.rule: Every control necessary for completing a task must be visibly accessible. A control the user cannot find does not exist — it will not be used, and the feature it enables will be considered absent from the product.
knowledge.detail: Hiding controls to create a visual impression of simplicity — the 'Illusion of Simplicity' — is a direct trade-off of user productivity for aesthetics. Hidden scroll bars, gesture-only interactions, hover-only reveals, and menus buried under non-obvious icons reduce functionality to zero for users who cannot discover them. This is distinct from progressive disclosure, where complexity is revealed as users need it — truly hidden controls are not revealed at all without prior knowledge or documentation. The test is simple: can a new user find the control without external help?
detection_signals: Check for interactive elements that are visually identical to non-interactive content — clickable text with no underline, color, or affordance signal is a discoverability failure
Flag icon-only controls when the icon is non-standard or ambiguous — an icon the user must guess at may as well not exist; text labels should accompany non-universal icons
Check for primary actions or settings buried under non-obvious parent items with no visual hint at their existence
Look for controls that only appear on hover — hover-only reveals are invisible on mobile and to users who do not explore systematically
Check if key actions in the current context are visible without scrolling or expanding — required controls should not need seeking
knowledge.vector_embedding_text: UX usability rule: every control necessary for completing a task must be visibly accessible. This falls under the ux-usability category, control-discoverability subcategory. Hiding controls to create the 'Illusion of Simplicity' trades user productivity for aesthetics — a hidden control is a non-existent control. This is distinct from progressive disclosure. The test: can a new user find the control without external help? To detect: check for interactive elements visually identical to non-interactive content; flag ambiguous icon-only controls with no text label; check for primary actions buried under non-obvious menus; look for hover-only reveals invisible on mobile; verify key actions are visible without scrolling. High severity. Applies across both viewport and full-page.

knowledge.rule: The size of a control must reflect its importance. Primary actions should be the largest interactive targets in their context; destructive or undesired actions should be smallest and physically separated. Target acquisition time increases with distance and decreases with size — small primary buttons impose a measurable usability cost.
knowledge.detail: Fitts's Law states that the time to acquire a target is a function of its distance and size. In UI design, large targets are faster to interact with — making primary actions large is not just visual hierarchy, it is a functional performance requirement. WCAG's 44×44px minimum touch target is an accessibility floor, not a design ceiling. Primary actions should generously exceed this minimum. Destructive actions — Delete, Remove, Downgrade — should be smaller, lower-contrast, or separated by space to reduce accidental activation. Equal-sized primary and destructive actions are a design error: they imply equal importance and increase the risk of irreversible mistakes.
detection_signals: Check that the primary CTA is the largest button in its immediate context — if a secondary or destructive action is equal size or larger, the sizing hierarchy is wrong
Flag primary and secondary buttons within the same component that are identical in size — equal sizing implies equal importance
Check for destructive actions (Delete, Cancel subscription, Remove) that share the same size and style as primary actions — they should be visually subordinate and physically separated
Check forms: the Submit or primary action button should be clearly more prominent than Cancel or Back controls
On mobile: primary actions should be generous beyond the 44×44px WCAG accessibility minimum — undersized primary buttons signal low priority and increase tap errors
knowledge.vector_embedding_text: UX usability rule: control size must reflect importance. This falls under the ux-usability category, fitts-law-sizing subcategory. Fitts's Law states acquisition time is a function of target size and distance — large targets are faster and easier to hit. Primary actions should be the largest interactive targets in context; destructive actions should be smaller and separated. WCAG's 44×44px minimum is an accessibility floor, not a design ceiling. Equal-sized primary and destructive actions are a design error. To detect: check primary CTA is largest button in context; flag equal-sized primary and secondary buttons; check destructive actions for visual subordination and separation; check form submit prominence; verify mobile primary actions generously exceed the 44×44px minimum. Medium severity. Applies across both viewport and full-page.

knowledge.rule: Elements that behave differently must look different. Applying the same visual style to controls with different behaviors misleads users into forming incorrect mental models. The inverse holds equally: elements that look the same must behave the same.
knowledge.detail: Visual consistency allows users to build accurate mental models — they learn that things looking like X do X. When an element looks like a button but navigates rather than submits, or looks like a link but triggers a destructive action, users are deceived by their own prior learning. This applies to the full interactive vocabulary: icons, buttons, links, and states. An icon commonly understood to mean one thing (gear = settings, trash = recoverable deletion) used for a different function undermines both the user's model and every other designer relying on that convention.
detection_signals: Check that buttons within a category behave similarly — navigation buttons, submission buttons, and destructive buttons should each have a visually distinct style
Flag cases where links and buttons use the same visual treatment despite serving different roles (navigation vs. action)
Check icon usage against common conventions: magnifying glass = search, gear = settings, trash = recoverable delete — deviations from these conventions are consistency violations
Look for primary and destructive buttons using identical visual styles — a 'Delete account' button that looks the same as 'Save changes' will cause accidental destructive actions

Check if interactive affordances are applied consistently across the page — if some clickable elements look like buttons and others are plain unstyled text with no affordance, the interactive vocabulary is broken
knowledge.vector_embedding_text: UX usability rule: elements that behave differently must look different — and elements that look the same must behave the same. This falls under the ux-usability category, visual-behavioral-consistency subcategory. Visual consistency builds accurate mental models. Applying the same style to controls with different behaviors deceives users via their own prior learning. This applies to the full interactive vocabulary: buttons, links, icons, states. Icon conventions (gear = settings, trash = recoverable delete) must not be violated. To detect: check that navigation, submission, and destructive buttons each have distinct styles; flag links and buttons sharing visual treatment despite different roles; check icon conventions for deviations; look for primary and destructive buttons styled identically; verify consistent interactive affordance signals across the page. Medium severity. Applies across both viewport and full-page.

knowledge.rule: When a page contains multiple landmarks of the same type — such as more than one navigation, region, or form — each must have a unique accessible label describing its purpose. Labels must never repeat the landmark role name.
knowledge.detail: Assistive technologies navigate web pages primarily through landmarks. When two navigation regions exist with no distinguishing label, AT users cannot tell them apart and may skip the wrong one entirely. Material Design 3 specifies: add clear and specific labels to any landmark roles that appear multiple times; never repeat the role name in the label — 'navigation navigation' is not a label, it is a duplication. All region landmarks should have labels; other repeated landmarks benefit from labels whenever a label adds meaning beyond the role name alone.
detection_signals: DOM: find repeated landmark roles — multiple nav, [role='navigation'], [role='region'], or [role='form'] elements on the same page
Flag repeated landmarks with no aria-label or aria-labelledby
Flag labels that include the landmark role name — 'navigation navigation', 'search search' — role is auto-announced by AT and must not be repeated in the label
Flag labels that are generic or positional rather than descriptive — 'nav 1', 'second menu' — these do not help users understand purpose
Positive signal: unique descriptive labels such as 'primary navigation', 'pagination', 'site search', 'contact form' on repeated landmark types
knowledge.vector_embedding_text: Accessibility rule: when a page contains multiple landmarks of the same type, each must have a unique accessible label describing its purpose. Labels must never repeat the landmark role name. This falls under the accessibility category, landmark-labeling subcategory. AT users navigate primarily through landmarks — unlabeled duplicate nav or region elements are indistinguishable. Material Design 3 specifies: add specific labels to repeated landmark roles; never repeat the role in the label. To detect: find repeated nav, region, or form elements in DOM; flag missing aria-label; flag labels that contain the role name; flag generic positional labels. Positive signal: unique descriptive labels on each repeated landmark type. Full-page scope. High severity.

knowledge.rule: Headings must reflect real content hierarchy, not visual styling. No heading levels should be skipped. Each page should have one h1. Heading tags must not be chosen based on preferred font size.
knowledge.detail: AT users navigate web pages primarily through headings — they form a traversable outline of the document. When heading levels are skipped (h2 followed by h4), the outline is broken and AT users lose their place. When h3 is used instead of h2 because it renders at a preferred visual size, the heading serves styling rather than structure. Material Design 3: 'Identify headings based on content hierarchy, rather than visual styling. Headings should not skip a level. A single h1 for the page title is recommended.' A page with multiple h1s (unless structured with explicit sectioning elements) fragments the primary orientation point for AT users.
detection_signals: DOM: extract h1–h6 elements in source order and check for skipped levels — h2 followed by h4, h1 followed by h3, etc.
Flag pages with no h1 when the page clearly has a primary title or dominant heading
Flag pages with multiple h1 elements not justified by explicit document sectioning
Flag heading tags that appear mismatched to their visual prominence — an h4 at the very top of a page followed by h2s is likely a visual-styling violation
Positive signal: headings form a clean sequential hierarchy (h1 → h2 → h3) that maps to the visible content structure without gaps
knowledge.vector_embedding_text: Accessibility rule: headings must reflect real content hierarchy, not visual styling. No levels should be skipped; each page should have one h1; heading tags must not be chosen for preferred font size. This falls under the accessibility category, heading-structure subcategory. AT users navigate through headings as a document outline — skipped levels break the outline and misplace users. Material Design 3: identify headings based on content hierarchy, not visual styling; do not skip levels; recommend a single h1. To detect: extract h1–h6 in source order; flag skipped levels; flag missing or multiple h1s; flag headings mismatched to visual prominence. Full-page scope. High severity.

knowledge.rule: Interactive targets placed adjacent to each other should have at least 8px of space between them. Spacing between targets is independent of target size — adequately sized targets still cause misactivation when packed too closely.
knowledge.detail: Material Design 3: 'In most cases, targets separated by 8dp of space or more promote balanced information density and usability.' Touch targets often extend invisibly beyond the visual element — a 24dp icon may have a 48dp touch area — and when those invisible areas overlap between adjacent elements, the wrong element activates. This is most common in icon rows, toolbars, pagination controls, social icon clusters, and footer link groups. This rule is distinct from kb-hig-006 (which addresses misclick risk between functionally-opposed actions); this addresses general proximity across any adjacent interactive targets.
detection_signals: DOM: measure the gap between bounding boxes of adjacent interactive elements — flag gaps smaller than 8px
Check icon rows, toolbars, social media link clusters, and pagination controls for tightly packed elements
Check footer link groups — a common failure location where social icons and legal links cluster with insufficient spacing
Screenshot: look for groups of icons or small controls with no visible gap between them
Treat this as a usability threshold, not a hard WCAG fail — raise severity when clustered controls perform destructive or irreversible actions
knowledge.vector_embedding_text: Accessibility rule: interactive targets placed adjacent to each other should have at least 8px of space between them. This falls under the accessibility category, target-spacing subcategory. Material Design 3 specifies 8dp spacing promotes balanced information density and usability. Touch targets extend invisibly beyond the visible element — adjacent targets with overlapping invisible touch areas cause misactivation. Most common failure locations: icon rows, toolbars, pagination, social icon clusters, footer link groups. Distinct from kb-hig-006 (functionally-opposed action spacing) — this covers general adjacency across any interactive targets. Applies across both viewport and full-page. Medium severity.

knowledge.rule: Essential text must not exist only inside a raster image. Screen readers cannot read image-embedded text. If an image contains essential information as text, that same information must appear in visible page text, a caption, or the image's alt text.
knowledge.detail: Material Design 3: 'Screen readers are unable to read text that is embedded in imagery. If there is essential information embedded as text in the image, include the essential information in the alt text.' Promotional banners, marketing hero images, infographic overlays, and event announcement cards commonly embed their primary message as image text. Users with images disabled, slow connections, high-zoom text settings, or who rely on AT lose this information entirely. The fix is not to remove the image — it is to ensure the essential content is also available in real text.
detection_signals: Screenshot: look for meaningful text rendered inside images — promotional banners with offer text, infographic labels, cards where the headline is baked into the image asset
DOM: check whether the same information appears nearby in visible text, a figcaption, or alt text on that image
Flag hero images or promo blocks where the primary message (headline, offer, CTA text) exists only within the image with no visible page text equivalent
Flag diagrams or charts with labeled data points that have no equivalent visible text or table
Exempt: decorative text used purely as a visual element when the same information is clearly present in nearby visible copy
knowledge.vector_embedding_text: Accessibility rule: essential text must not exist only inside a raster image — screen readers cannot read image-embedded text. This falls under the accessibility category, embedded-text-in-images subcategory. Material Design 3: include essential image-embedded text in alt text or visible page text. Promotional banners, hero images, infographics, and event cards commonly fail this. Users with images disabled, AT users, or users at high zoom lose the information entirely. To detect: look for meaningful text inside images in the screenshot; check if that information exists in visible copy, figcaption, or alt text; flag promo heroes and infographics where the primary message is image-only. Applies across both viewport and full-page. High severity.

knowledge.rule: Informative images should be accompanied by a caption or adjacent text that explains their relevance to the surrounding content. An image whose purpose is not explained by nearby copy forces the user to infer meaning alone.
knowledge.detail: Material Design 3: 'Captions explain contextual information about the image to help the reader understand how it relates to the content. Both sighted and screen reader users rely on captions.' An informative image — a diagram, product photo, chart, article illustration — earns its place by conveying meaning that enhances the text. When no caption or adjacent copy explains what the image shows and why it is there, users must interpret it independently. This is particularly common in AI-generated UIs where images are inserted as visual filler rather than communicative elements. Decorative images used purely for atmosphere or texture are exempt.
detection_signals: Screenshot: identify informative images — diagrams, charts, article illustrations, product photos, instructional visuals — and check for accompanying caption or adjacent explanatory text
DOM: look for a figcaption element, an adjacent paragraph, or a nearby heading that explains the image's relevance
Flag images that carry clear meaning but have no surrounding explanatory text — the image sits alone with no context as to why it is there
Positive signal: a caption or adjacent text explains what the image shows and why it matters in the current context
Exempt: abstract backgrounds, texture fills, and purely decorative photos that add atmosphere without conveying specific information
