knowledge.vector_embedding_text: Content copy principle: informative images should be accompanied by caption or adjacent text explaining their relevance. This falls under the content-copy category, image-context subcategory. Material Design 3: captions help both sighted and AT users understand how the image relates to the content. Images without context force users to infer meaning independently — a common failure in AI-generated UIs where images serve as visual filler. To detect: identify informative images (diagrams, charts, illustrations, product photos); check for figcaption, adjacent paragraph, or heading explaining the image; flag context-free informative images. Exempt: decorative images with no specific information. Full-page scope. Medium severity.

knowledge.rule: Truncated text is only acceptable if users can access the full content through a recovery path — a tooltip, an expanded view, or a link. An ellipsis with no recovery mechanism makes the truncated content permanently inaccessible.
knowledge.detail: Material Design 3: 'If there's an ellipsis, but no way to show the truncated text, it is not accessible.' Truncation is sometimes necessary when space is constrained, but the truncated content must always remain accessible through one of three patterns: (1) a tooltip on hover/focus revealing the full text, (2) a link or interaction that expands the full content, (3) tapping or clicking the element opens a full view. Truncation with none of these is not a brevity technique — it is content removal. This applies especially to button labels: a truncated button label communicates nothing about the action.
detection_signals: Screenshot: look for visible ellipses (...) in labels, headings, card titles, table cells, navigation items, and button labels
Flag any truncated text where no recovery mechanism is apparent — no tooltip indicator, no expand control, no clickable state leading to full content
Flag button labels clipped with an ellipsis — a truncated button label is always a failure; buttons must show their full intent
Check table cells, list items, and card titles for ellipsis truncation without a clearly linked full view
Positive signal: clipped text has a tooltip, 'Read more' link, or a clearly interactive affordance indicating the full content is accessible
knowledge.vector_embedding_text: Content copy rule: truncated text is only acceptable if users can access the full content through a tooltip, expanded view, or link. This falls under the content-copy category, truncation-recovery subcategory. Material Design 3: an ellipsis with no recovery path is not accessible. Truncation is sometimes necessary but the content must always remain reachable. Truncated button labels are always a failure — buttons must show their full intent. To detect: look for ellipses in labels, headings, card titles, table cells, and buttons; flag any truncated text with no visible recovery mechanism; flag all truncated button labels. Positive signal: clipped text has tooltip, expand control, or linked full view. Applies across both viewport and full-page. Medium severity.

knowledge.rule: Accessibility labels on icons, images, and interactive elements must describe what the element does or means — not what it looks like. The element's role must not be included in the label, as the browser announces it automatically.
knowledge.detail: Material Design 3: 'The accessibility labels for these icons describe their purpose — NOT what the icon looks like (for example, magnifying glass).' A label of 'Magnifying glass' tells a screen reader user what is rendered, not what it does. 'Search' is complete. Adding the role compounds the problem: a button labeled 'Settings button' announces as 'Settings button button' — the role is auto-appended by the browser and repeating it creates a redundant double-announcement. Labels should be concise, action-oriented or meaning-oriented, and role-free.
detection_signals: DOM: inspect aria-label, alt text, title attributes, and aria-labelledby text for icon buttons and meaningful images

Flag labels that describe icon appearance rather than purpose — 'magnifying glass' instead of 'Search', 'three dots' instead of 'More options', 'gear' instead of 'Settings', 'hamburger' instead of 'Open menu'
Flag labels containing role words — 'Settings button', 'Navigation menu', 'Search icon' — role is auto-announced by AT and repeating it causes a double-announcement
Check progress indicators, status icons, and data visualisations for meaningful accessible names — 'Loading' is acceptable; 'spinning circle' is not
Positive signal: labels describe action or outcome — 'Open settings', 'Search', 'More options', 'Go to homepage'
knowledge.vector_embedding_text: Accessibility rule: labels on icons and interactive elements must describe what the element does, not what it looks like, and must not include the element role. This falls under the accessibility category, label-purpose subcategory. Material Design 3: labels describe purpose, not appearance — not 'magnifying glass' but 'Search'. Role is auto-announced by AT; including it in the label causes double-announcement ('Settings button button'). To detect: inspect aria-label, alt text, and title attributes; flag appearance-descriptive labels; flag labels containing role words; check progress indicators and status icons for meaningful names. Positive signal: labels describe action or outcome. Applies across both viewport and full-page. Medium severity.

knowledge.rule: All-caps must never be used for running text, dialog content, helper text, instructions, or any multi-word text that is not a short intentional label. Caps blocks — extended all-caps text beyond a label — are an accessibility anti-pattern that reduces legibility and can cause inconsistent screen reader behaviour.
knowledge.detail: Material Design 3 explicitly classifies caps blocks as an anti-pattern: 'Avoid using caps blocks altogether; they're not accessible.' The accessibility concern is distinct from the readability concern: CSS text-transform: uppercase does not change the underlying text content, but some screen reader and text-to-speech combinations respond differently to visually transformed uppercase text. The readability failure is absolute — all-caps eliminates the distinctive word shapes that enable rapid reading, replacing them with uniform-height rectangles. Short intentional labels (overlines, compact utility labels, navigation items of 1–3 words) are the only acceptable use. See also kb-ty-005 for the typography-angle detection of the same pattern.
detection_signals: Screenshot: identify extended all-caps text — more than 4–5 consecutive words in any non-label context
Flag dialog body text, helper text, form instructions, tooltips, and error messages rendered in all-caps
DOM: flag text-transform: uppercase on containers holding paragraph, helper, dialog, or instructional text
Acceptable all-caps contexts: overlines above headlines, compact utility labels, navigation items of 1–3 words — verify the flagged element does not fall into these categories before raising
Flag all-caps headings spanning more than one line — this combines the typography violation (kb-ty-005) with the accessibility anti-pattern; raise severity accordingly
knowledge.vector_embedding_text: Content copy rule: all-caps must never be used for running text, dialog content, helper text, or any multi-word text beyond a short intentional label. This falls under the content-copy category, caps-block-antipattern subcategory. Material Design 3: avoid caps blocks altogether — they are not accessible. CSS text-transform: uppercase can cause inconsistent screen reader behaviour; all-caps also eliminates word shape recognition, slowing reading significantly. Acceptable: overlines, compact utility labels, navigation items of 1–3 words. To detect: identify extended all-caps text beyond label contexts; flag dialog, helper, instruction, and error text in all-caps; check DOM for text-transform: uppercase on paragraph or instructional containers. See also kb-ty-005. Full-page scope. Medium severity.

knowledge.rule: A UI should use no more than 2–3 distinct typeface families. Mixing more typefaces obscures information hierarchy, makes the interface feel inconsistent, and signals a lack of design intent.
knowledge.detail: Each additional typeface creates a new visual language the user must parse. When 4+ families appear on a single page — common in AI-generated UIs that pull from multiple component libraries — no single typographic voice emerges and the hierarchy becomes noise. The only exception is monospace for code display, which is functionally distinct. Brand fonts for display headings + system font for body is the canonical two-family pattern.
detection_signals: Count distinct font families visible across the full page — detect serif, sans-serif, display, and monospace families separately
Flag if more than 3 distinct font families appear (not counting monospace used specifically for code display)
Check if the same typeface is used at different optical settings that make it appear as a different font (e.g. compressed vs. expanded variants) — this is not a typeface violation
AI-generated UIs frequently mix the component library's default font with a separately imported brand font and a third utility font — flag this combination
knowledge.vector_embedding_text: Typography rule: a UI should use no more than 2–3 distinct typeface families. This falls under the typography category, typeface-count subcategory. Mixing more than 3 typeface families obscures information hierarchy, fragments the visual voice, and makes the interface feel inconsistently designed. The canonical pattern is a brand or display font for headings and a system font for body copy. AI-generated UIs frequently pull components from multiple libraries, each carrying its own default font. To detect: count distinct font families across the full page; flag if more than 3 appear; monospace for code display is the accepted exception. Full-page scope. Medium severity.

knowledge.rule: A color used to signal interactivity must not also be used for decorative or non-interactive elements. Using the same accent color on both clickable buttons and decorative text or backgrounds destroys the color's ability to signal 'this is tappable.'
knowledge.detail: Color is one of the primary signals users rely on to identify interactive elements. When an accent color appears on a primary CTA button and also on a decorative heading, a pull quote, or a background illustration, the signal is broken — users cannot confidently distinguish what is interactive from what is not. This is particularly damaging for borderless or flat-design buttons that rely solely on color for their interactive affordance. The rule also applies to status colors: red/green/amber used for warnings or success states must not appear on neutral decorative elements.
detection_signals: Identify the primary brand/accent color used on the most prominent CTA buttons
Check whether that same color (or a very similar hue) appears on text, illustrations, borders, or backgrounds that are not interactive
Flag instances where the CTA color appears on decorative heading text or section backgrounds — this breaks the interactive affordance signal
Check status colors (red for error, green for success): if these appear on decorative or promotional elements unrelated to system status, flag it
Borderless buttons (no visible border, color-only affordance) are highest risk — if their color appears elsewhere non-interactively, they lose all affordance
knowledge.vector_embedding_text: Color rule: a color used to signal interactivity must not also appear on decorative or non-interactive elements. This falls under the contrast-color category, semantic-color-consistency subcategory. When an accent color used on CTA buttons also appears on decorative headings, pull quotes, or section backgrounds, users cannot distinguish interactive from non-interactive elements. This is highest risk for borderless flat-design buttons that rely on color alone for their affordance. Status colors (red, green, amber) must not appear on elements unrelated to system feedback. To detect: identify the CTA accent color; flag that color appearing on non-interactive text, borders, or backgrounds; check status colors on decorative elements. Full-page scope. High severity.

knowledge.rule: Button and CTA labels must begin with an action verb that clearly describes the outcome. Labels that are noun-only, vague ('Click here', 'Submit', 'Go'), or cleverly indirect ('Let's do it!') reduce clarity and harm screen reader usability.
knowledge.detail: Verb-first labels make the outcome of pressing the button explicit before the user commits. 'Download the Guide' is unambiguous; 'Get it here' requires the user to remember context from surrounding copy. 'Click here' is the worst case — it describes the mechanism (clicking), not the action, and is meaningless when read by a screen reader out of context. Overly cute labels trade clarity for personality at the expense of comprehension. The standard is: a user reading the button label alone must know what happens when they press it.
detection_signals: Check all visible button and CTA labels for action verbs — flag labels that are noun-only (e.g., 'Options', 'Details') or mechanism-only ('Click here', 'Learn more' without context)
Flag labels that rely entirely on surrounding context to be meaningful — 'Get it', 'Yes!', 'Start' without a direct object
Check primary CTAs in the viewport: the most important button should have the most explicit label
Flag decorative or aspirational labels that replace clarity with brand voice: 'Let's go!', 'Let's do it!', 'Make it happen' — these obscure what actually happens
In forms: check submit buttons for specificity — 'Send Message' is clearer than 'Submit'; 'Create Account' is clearer than 'Done'
knowledge.vector_embedding_text: Content copy rule: button and CTA labels must begin with an action verb that clearly describes the outcome. This falls under the content-copy category, button-label-verbs subcategory. Verb-first labels make the result of a tap or click explicit before the user commits. 'Download the Guide' is unambiguous; 'Click here' describes only the mechanism and is meaningless when read by a screen reader. Overly clever labels like 'Let's do it!' trade clarity for personality. The test: reading the button label alone, the user must know what happens. To detect: flag noun-only, mechanism-only, or context-dependent button labels; check primary CTAs for explicit verb phrases; check form submit buttons for specificity. Applies across both viewport and full-page. Medium severity.

knowledge.rule: UI copy must apply a consistent capitalization style within each element type across the full interface. Mixing title case and sentence case on buttons, or switching between heading styles mid-page, signals inconsistent production and erodes the perception of quality.
knowledge.detail: Capitalization choice (title case vs. sentence case) is a style decision, and neither is objectively superior. The rule is consistency: all navigation labels, all buttons, all section headings within the same level should follow the same pattern. AI-generated UIs frequently produce mixed capitalization because different components were generated with different prompts or at different times. A navigation bar with 'About Us', 'Our Work', and 'contact us' in the same row is a detectable inconsistency. Mixed styles are most common in: button labels, navigation items, card headings, and form field labels.
detection_signals: Read all button labels visible on the page — check whether they consistently use title case or sentence case; flag any mixing
Check navigation menu items (header and footer) — all items at the same level should use the same capitalization style
Check section headings at the same hierarchy level (e.g., all H2s) — flag if some use title case and others sentence case
Check form field labels for consistency — 'First Name' (title) vs. 'First name' (sentence) mixed in the same form is a flag
Note: ALL CAPS headers are a distinct style choice and can be consistent; the violation is mixing ALL CAPS with non-caps in the same element type
knowledge.vector_embedding_text: Content copy rule: UI copy must apply a consistent capitalization style within each element type across the full interface. This falls under the content-copy category, capitalization-consistency subcategory. Neither title case nor sentence case is wrong — inconsistency between them within the same element type is the violation. AI-generated UIs frequently produce mixed capitalization because different sections were generated independently. The most common locations for violation are button labels, navigation items, section headings, and form field labels. To detect: read all button labels and check for consistent capitalization style; check navigation items at the same level; check H2s across the page; check form labels. Full-page scope. Low severity.

knowledge.rule: Error messages must appear adjacent to the source of the error, be specific about what went wrong, and tell the user exactly what to do to fix it. Errors displayed only at the top of a form, generic messages ('Something went wrong'), or blame-placing language ('Invalid input') all fail this standard.
knowledge.detail: Error messages are the most consequential copy in any form or transaction flow — they appear exactly when a user is frustrated, and their quality directly determines whether the user corrects the error or abandons. Apple HIG specifies: display close to the problem, avoid blame, be clear about the fix. 'That password is too short' fails: it names the problem but not the solution. 'Choose a password with at least 8 characters' is complete — it names the problem and prescribes the specific fix. Error modals that appear far from the triggering field add cognitive load. Interjections ('Oops!', 'Uh-oh') are tone-deaf in high-frustration contexts.
detection_signals: Check inline error messages in visible form states — are they adjacent to their field or only displayed at the top of the form?
Read visible error messages: does each one name both the problem AND the specific fix? Flag messages that name only the problem
Flag generic error messages: 'Something went wrong', 'Invalid input', 'Error', 'Please try again' without further detail
Flag error messages that use blame language (e.g., 'You entered an invalid date') vs. neutral instructional language ('Enter the date as DD/MM/YYYY')
Flag error modals or toast notifications that don't specify which field or action triggered them
Check for interjections ('Oops!', 'Uh-oh', 'Yikes!') in error contexts — these are inappropriate in high-frustration moments
knowledge.vector_embedding_text: Content copy rule: error messages must appear adjacent to their source, be specific about the problem, and provide a precise fix. This falls under the content-copy category, error-message-quality subcategory. Error messages appear when users are most frustrated — their quality determines whether the user corrects or abandons. A complete error message names the problem and prescribes the specific action: 'Choose a password with at least 8 characters' not 'Password too short.' Errors shown only at the top of forms, generic text like 'Something went wrong,' blame language, or interjections like 'Oops!' all fail. To detect: check inline error placement; read error text for problem+fix completeness; flag generic, blame, or interjection language. Applies across both viewport and full-page. High severity.

knowledge.rule: Interactive controls must have sufficient padding between each other to prevent accidental activation. For controls with visible bezels/borders, approximately 12px minimum gap; for controls without a visible border, approximately 24px gap. Closely packed controls cause misclick errors.
knowledge.detail: Spacing between interactive controls is as critical as the size of each control. When buttons, links, or form elements are positioned too close together, touch or click accuracy degrades and users frequently activate the wrong control. Apple HIG specifies approximately 12pt around bezeled elements and approximately 24pt around non-bezeled elements. In web pixels at standard DPI these translate to approximately 16px and 24px respectively. Critical above the fold where primary actions are clustered, and on mobile where a finger's contact area is larger than the visible tap target.
detection_signals: Check button groups (segmented controls, adjacent CTA + secondary button) for visible gap — gap narrower than approximately 12px is a likely misclick risk
Check icon-only controls (close buttons, social icons, nav items) — these commonly cluster with insufficient spacing
On mobile screenshot: check bottom-of-screen action areas where CTAs and secondary actions appear in close proximity
Check form submit buttons and cancel/back links — a 'Submit' button placed immediately adjacent to a 'Delete' or 'Cancel' with no gap is a destructive misclick risk
DOM: check padding and margin between adjacent interactive elements (buttons, links, inputs) — compute the gap between each pair's bounding boxes
knowledge.vector_embedding_text: Spacing layout rule: interactive controls must have sufficient padding between each other to prevent accidental activation. This falls under the spacing-layout category, control-spacing subcategory. Apple HIG specifies approximately 12px around bezeled elements and 24px around non-bezeled elements. Closely packed controls cause touch and click misactivation — destructive when adjacent actions have very different outcomes (Submit vs. Cancel). Most critical above the fold and on mobile where touch contact areas are large. To detect: check button groups for visible gap; check icon clusters; check mobile action areas; check adjacent Submit and Cancel pairs; DOM: compute gap between adjacent interactive element bounding boxes. Applies across both viewport and full-page. Medium severity.

knowledge.rule: Brand assets (logos, wordmarks) should appear at most once per view in a clearly designated location (typically top-left navigation). Repeating a logo or wordmark multiple times across a single screen displaces content and signals a lack of design maturity.
knowledge.detail: Users rarely need to be reminded which product they are using. Screen space is finite, and every pixel given to a repeated logo is taken from content, hierarchy, or breathing room. Apple HIG explicitly states: 'Resist the temptation to display your logo throughout your app unless it's essential for providing context.' In web UI, the violation most commonly appears as: logo in the navigation + logo in the hero section — two high-prominence appearances in the above-fold area. Footer placement is conventional and does not count as a duplication.
detection_signals: Count logo/wordmark appearances in the above-fold area — flag if the logo appears more than once above the fold (nav + hero duplication is the most common form)
Check hero sections: is the logo or wordmark repeated immediately below the navigation bar that already contains it?
Check if a brand watermark or logo appears over product imagery as a design element separate from the navigation logo
Note: favicon, browser tab, and footer logo repetition are conventional and acceptable. The violation is above-fold duplication.
Check whether repeated brand assets displace the primary value proposition or push the CTA below the fold
knowledge.vector_embedding_text: Visual hierarchy principle: brand assets should appear at most once in a primary navigation location per view. This falls under the visual-hierarchy category, branding-content-ratio subcategory. Users already know which product they are using — repeated logos take space from content. Apple HIG: resist displaying logos throughout the UI unless essential for context. The most common violation: logo in navigation + logo in hero section on the same view. Footer repetition is conventional and accepted. To detect: count logo appearances above the fold — flag more than one primary-context appearance; check if hero section repeats the nav logo; check if brand watermarks overlay product imagery. Full-page scope. Low severity.

knowledge.rule: UI motion and animation must serve a specific communicative purpose. Looping animations, auto-playing video without controls, and rapid blinking elements in the viewport increase cognitive load and can trigger photosensitivity responses. Motion should never be the primary channel for critical information.
knowledge.detail: Gratuitous animation distracts users from content, increases cognitive load for all users, and poses accessibility risks including photosensitive epilepsy for elements flashing at 3+ Hz. Apple HIG states: 'Don't add motion for the sake of adding motion.' Critical information conveyed only through animation (a loading spinner with no text, a progress bar with no percentage) excludes users with motion-sensitive conditions and is lost when Reduce Motion is enabled. Autoplay video with audio hijacks the user's attention without consent and is a known trust-damager.
detection_signals: Check the viewport for elements that appear to be animated (looping illustrations, particle effects, animated backgrounds, auto-playing video) — detectable from motion blur artifacts or video player UI in the screenshot
Check for autoplay video indicators in the viewport — most platforms show a play/pause control when video is present
Flag hero sections where the primary visual is an animation or video with no apparent static fallback
Flag loading or processing states visible in the screenshot where the only indicator is a spinner with no text status beside it
Check for elements that appear to use rapid contrast changes or strobing patterns — high-frequency white-to-black alternation is a photosensitivity risk
knowledge.vector_embedding_text: Accessibility principle: UI motion and animation must serve a specific communicative purpose and must not be the sole channel for critical information. This falls under the accessibility category, motion-cognitive-load subcategory. Looping animations, autoplay video, and rapid blinking elements increase cognitive load and can cause photosensitive responses. Apple HIG: do not add motion for the sake of motion. Critical information conveyed only through animation is inaccessible when Reduce Motion is enabled. Autoplay video with audio is a consent violation and trust-damager. To detect: check viewport for animated elements (hero animations, looping backgrounds, autoplay video); flag loading states with no text status; flag high-contrast alternating patterns. Viewport scope. Medium severity.

knowledge.rule: Empty states — views where a list, dashboard, or content area has no data — must explain why the state is empty and provide a clear action to populate it or get started. A blank area with no guidance leaves users stranded.
knowledge.detail: Empty states occur throughout a product: an empty inbox, a dashboard with no connected data, a search result with no matches, a completed task list. The user who encounters an empty state is either new (they haven't yet added data) or at a dead end (a search returned nothing). Both require immediate guidance: what is this area for, and what should I do now? The best empty states combine a brief explanation with a direct action button. 'No projects yet — Create your first project [button]' is complete. A blank area with only a generic illustration and no text or CTA is a stranded user experience. Search no-results states are especially critical — they must offer alternative suggestions or a fallback action.
detection_signals: Check for visually empty content areas (tables with no rows, empty card grids, blank inbox-style layouts) — does each have explanatory text and an action?
Flag large white or background-colored areas with only a generic illustration and no text or action button
Check search or filter results that visibly show 'no results' — is there a suggestion, alternate query, or next-step action?
Check dashboard-style layouts where data widgets show placeholder states — do placeholder states explain the empty condition and provide a next step?
On first-run / onboarding views: does the empty state guide the user toward the first meaningful action in the product?
knowledge.vector_embedding_text: UX usability rule: empty states must explain why the view is empty and provide a clear action to populate it. This falls under the ux-usability category, empty-state-guidance subcategory. Users encountering empty states are either new (no data yet) or at a dead end (no search results) — both need immediate guidance. Complete empty states combine an explanation with an action button: 'No projects yet — Create your first project.' A blank area with only an illustration and no text or CTA is a failure. Search no-results states must offer suggestions or fallback actions. To detect: check visually empty content areas for explanatory text and action; flag blank illustration-only cards; check no-results states; check placeholder widgets on dashboards. Viewport scope. Medium severity.

knowledge.rule: In left-to-right reading interfaces, the most important content and primary CTA must be positioned in the upper half of the viewport, visible without scrolling. Placing the value proposition below decorative elements, or the primary CTA below the viewport midpoint on first load, imposes a scroll cost that directly reduces conversion.
knowledge.detail: Reading order is the default cognitive map users apply to any new screen: top to bottom, leading to trailing. Apple HIG: 'place the most important items near the top and leading side of the window.' This is consistent with F-pattern and layer-cake scanning research — the top-left quadrant is the highest-attention zone for LTR interfaces. AI-generated UIs that use fully centered symmetric layouts often fail this principle by giving equal spatial priority to primary and secondary content, with no placement advantage asserted for the core message.
detection_signals: On the viewport screenshot: identify the primary value proposition (headline or hero statement) — is it positioned in the upper half of the viewport, visible without scrolling?
Identify the primary CTA button — is it visible without scrolling on the desktop viewport (and on mobile)?
Flag layouts where the primary CTA appears below the visual midpoint of the viewport on first load
Check if a large decorative illustration or image appears above the primary headline — this places decoration in the highest-attention zone ahead of the message
For fully centered layouts: check if the centered content uses size, color, or spacing to assert hierarchy between primary and secondary content, or treats all content with equal visual weight

This falls under the visual-hierarchy category, spatial-importance-placement subcategory. Reading order — top to bottom, left to right — is the default cognitive map for any new screen. The top-left quadrant is the highest-attention zone. CTAs below the viewport midpoint on first load impose a scroll cost that directly reduces conversion. AI-generated fully centered layouts often fail by treating primary and secondary content with equal spatial priority. To detect: check if the value proposition is in the upper viewport half; check if the primary CTA is visible without scrolling; flag decorative imagery stacked above the headline; check centered layouts for hierarchy absence. Viewport scope. Medium severity.
